author: mushow0x
content:
Hello <@755590043632140352> <@586161934425128960>. 
There was a finding that came in the bug bounty program a couple of months ago, but The PoC given was "gibberish", and super hard to read, mostly AI slop. so we ended up dropping it and the researcher never came back pushing for it. 

Recently one of our swarms though came back with a very similar bug, and has made it much more concise and included a consensus spec test. 

I was wondering if you could take a look, I know sometimes we can create stuff from an "impossible state" and it still passes. 

https://gist.github.com/0xMushow/064e2d89a517f9ebe6285ce9d09cfabc

author: potuz
content:
This is flawed:
> However, if the payment met quorum before eviction, epoch processing already appended payment.withdrawal to state.builder_pending_withdrawals.
There is no possibility of achieving quorum if there hasn't been any blocks from the parent until this block. Quorum is on-chain and no attestations could have been processed

author: potuz
content: I stopped reading here, but I assume you can get an AI to vet that the PoC starts with a flawed state in which the quorum is applied before the epoch transition, which I claim it's impossible

author: potuz
content: also notice that in general, quorum is irrelevant for a payload that **has been included**, the payment processed in this function is for payloads that have been included and thus we just charge   the builder

author: nflaig
content: concluded the same, the poc tests the reporter added does `_attest_parent_bid_to_quorum` which calls `process_attestation` but not actually processes a block

author: mushow0x
content: thanks for the quick analysis. it's so easy to build a test that passes but has invalid state

author: potuz
content: Yeah that's why fuzzing STF is hard
